There is one feature I was thinking about while reviewing that I think makes a lot of sense, so I wonder what you think about it: In my mind, the default use-case for reading audio is to read it alongside some video frames/stream and to then pass both the frame and the corresponding audio into some ML pipeline or deep learning model. Currently, there is no easy way for a user to figure out which segment of audio corresponds to a video frame stored at a given index. Do you think it would be useful to add code that allows reading/iterating over the audio in batches that correspond to the start and duration at which individual frames are shown?

A few years later, some review comments (I'm mostly looking at this from the POV of the Signer interface and  #447):

I think we should not expose all of this as a new module -- especially things like get_keys_on_hsm(), get_hsms() seem like things that should not be public API here

`HSMSigner` on the other hand sounds like a reasonable thing: The API would include 
* key and device ids as constructor arguments (optional if PyCKS supports that)
* PKCS module loaded using environment variable
* PIN as constructor variable

This would be compatible with #447 with a URI like `"hms:?slot=c9&device=0"`, and pin getting requested with `secrets_handler()`
 
The remaining functionality would then be :
* export_pubkey()

Interestingly this same issue comes up with KMS keys... Maybe _public key export_ could actually be a method on the Signer as well: not all implementations would support it but some could.

Another thing that comes up with KMS keys as well is the need to prehash the payload. We should add that support somewhere -- figuring out the algorithm from the public key is currently very annoying. Can we have `Key.get_payload_hash_algorithm()`?

Left also some code comments -- they may be obsolete so feel free to disregard

I think we moved the code to prototype to early (that's on me) before we confirm that all issues are fixed. Since the tests on prototypes are not executed on CI, we now risk not detecting issues with the implementation.

I propose undoing the move to prototype, running the tests on the CI and ensure everything works prior moving everything to the prototype again. I did this job on a separate no-merge PR at #4984 and as you can see the [tests fail](https://app.circleci.com/pipelines/github/pytorch/vision/12826/workflows/eb0ae338-2e22-44f5-ad0d-92dbc7716e62/jobs/1019329). You are welcome to merge my changes into your current PR to investigate.

2. For the schema, I pinged one of the engineers there. I prefer. timestamp & integer type, but I will wait for their response.

First of all, so exciting to see you back around here @dmontagu! 🤩🎉

## PR Review

Now to the PR review:

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [x] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [x] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [ ] ConfigDict fields
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [ ] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

Thanks for your interest in LightGBM and for taking the time to modify the documentation.

I'm ok with this change to the docs. However, we have a slightly different process for modifying the `Parameters` documentation page.

Please modify this:

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/4f47547c88cc72de4b8f04de8de4e2b79a9240e7/include/LightGBM/config.h#L336

Then run this from the root of the repo

```shell
python helpers/parameter_generator.py
```

and commit the resulting changes.

-------

In the future, I strongly recommend making pull requests from a new branch on your fork, instead of the target repo's default branch (`master`, in LightGBM's case). This project uses squash-merging, so `master` on your fork and `master` here in the main repository are going to have different histories now that you've created a pull request from `master` in your fork.

Once this pull request is merged, I recommend deleting your fork of LightGBM and then re-forking if you want to make further contributions here.

A couple high level points:
1. I think we should limit the amount we make modifications to the superclass Trainer inside the subclass Trainer, and try to fit in with the superclass APIs as much as possible. HFTrainer will serve as the primary example for how we integrate other Trainers in the future, so it would be great to leverage the APIs that we have built.
2. I also think we should limit the amount we are modifying Huggingface behavior and try to use their public APIs as much as possible, leave validation & error handling to them, and fail quickly if the user tries to do things that we don't support instead of overriding this behavior to get this to work. This will make sure our implementation is easy to maintain and is future-proof (after all they are working on transformers v5 and a lot of their APIs will be changing). This was a mistake I made with ray-lightning, and it's been a pain to maintain it, so hoping not to repeat this again! Good thing is, if their APIs are not flexible enough to support our use cases, we can work with the HF team to make the necessary changes.

looks great overall! just a couple of questions/nits

one broader questions i want to clarify is what assumptions we are making when accessing the payload of the request object.

if the request is a form request and contains a form-encoded body, the following calls will return
```python
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads from the stream, caches the body, and returns it
request.form                   # parses the form data from the cache and returns the form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # returns the cached body (will contain the original form-encoded body!)
````

whereas calling:
```python
request.data                   # reads from the stream, parses the form data, caches the form data
                               # and caches the remaining body (which is now empty, and will return `""`)
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
request.form                   # returns the cached form data
request.get_data(as_text=True) # reads and returns the cached body (which is empty)
```

see here for a detailed explanation: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/blob/bb21bf90b0b121e3ed45b9950b823e4b43a81fd8/src/werkzeug/wrappers/request.py#L385-L390

so that the parser will potentially behave differently depending on previous calls to `request.data` or `request.get_data(parse_form_data=True)`.
i'm not sure exactly how to address this.

after writing all this and thinking a bit more about the werkzeug API, i think it may be the best call to NOT call `get_data` at all, and just rely on the properties `request.data`, `request.values`, `request.form`, `request.args`, etc, which seems to be the way it was intended.
you lose the bit of convenience that `as_text=True` provides, but at then the behavior will be consistent.

First of all I see the value of this PR in terms of adding support for more segmentation use-cases. The part I'm concerned with is the direction of supporting float images as this forces us to expose unnecessarily internals of transforms such as the [0,1] normalization.

I think it might be beneficial to take a step back and consider: 
1. Is supporting floats absolutely necessary for this PR? Is it something can be removed from the PR to unblock it and facilitate further discussions on the topic?
2. Is the target of removing the integer version of the images worth it, given that in the examples we are still forced to keep a [normalized](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/efb12bc2a5260cd81cce38190886b6f48ae098ea/gallery/plot_visualization_utils.py#L125) and [non-normalized](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/efb12bc2a5260cd81cce38190886b6f48ae098ea/gallery/plot_visualization_utils.py#L79) version of the image? Also why try to remove the [integer version](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/3827#discussion_r631948664) if it's needed by some other methods?

Note that this PR needs to be assessed together with #3827 and #3824 to get a clearer picture. A potential better way to split this work into multiple PRs is to separate the change of behaviour of `draw_segmentation_masks()` and the proposal of adding float support into 2 separate PRs so that they don't block each other. Given the above, I can't merge this PR as is but I'm happy to reconsider if changes are made to address the concerns I raised.

Thanks for raising this PR! I like the algorithm and that it nicely generalizes the earlier solution. However, I'd like to propose a different code organization, so that the core module doesn't grow too large:

* Add a `reachability.py` module with a `Reachability` class.
* This class stores the no-fall-through-nodes, allows to reset them, has a `visit(node)` method that checks the node's type and depending on the type marks nodes as no-fall-through or checks whether their body is fully executable.
* The Vulture class has a Reachability member and calls its `visit(node)` function, and retrieves the unreachable code from Reachability before switching to the next module.

This goes in the direction of adding a second NodeVisitor class, but avoids traversing the AST twice. Or do you think a second NodeVisitor class (with all of these visitor_* functions) would be a better alternative?

I noticed that you made changes to `field.py` to only allow deprecation on nullable fields. According to the Spec, this constraint only applies to input fields. 
Look at the following example from spec:

```
type ExampleType {
  invalidField(
    newArg: String
    oldArg: String! @deprecated(reason: "Use `newArg`.")
  ): String
}
```
With input fields, the restriction makes sense, as we don't have any alternative to passing a value to the required `oldField`, although `oldField` is deprecated. For input fields, the alternative is to not query the field anymore.

This PR introduces a variety of different, distinct changes, that I have comments on individually. As such, I figured it might be better to write them here, rather than at specific lines.

### Increasing "filetype" intelligence

All for this. Yay! I was originally worried about the compatibility with the config transformer, but it looks like the config transformer just attempts all files anyway, so we should be fine.

### Keyword Detector Improvements

TBH, this file continues to mutate, and becomes increasingly hard to understand -- so it takes me a while to understand your changes. I like your `FOLLOWED_BY_COMPARATION_QUOTES_REQUIRED_REGEX` and `FOLLOWED_BY_REV_COMPARATION_QUOTES_REQUIRED_REGEX`, but I'm uncertain of the implications of modifying the core `DENYLIST_REGEX` and `SECRET` regexes.

It *should* be the case that secret values don't have quotes in them: but I need to think more deeply about whether we can assert this.

### KeywordXML

Ok, this absolutely needs to change. Along the same lines of why the KeywordDetector is in need for some serious love (to increase code flexibility), I'm against a direct copy-paste of the `KeywordDetector` to make a version for XML files.

Have you considered writing an XML [transformer](https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets/blob/pre-v1-launch/docs/design.md#transformers) instead? This will allow it to take advantage of all the other features that v1 provides. For prior art, check out [YAMLTransformer](https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets/blob/pre-v1-launch/detect_secrets/transformers/yaml.py#L25).

One concern I have is that the `MultiProcessDataLoader` is only being used to load `Instances`, and so we lose out on one of it's advantages: that indexing, padding, and batching can be done in worker processes, not the main process.

But by only using the `MultiProcessDataLoader` to call `iter_instances()`, `MultiTaskDataLoader` becomes responsible for indexing, padding, and batching, which then have to be done in the main process unless we add some extra multi-processing logic to `MultiTaskDataLoader`, which I think is a bad idea because that stuff is complicated.

Here's an alternative suggestion:

`MultiTaskDataLoader` becomes a very thin wrapper around `MultiProcessDataLoader` that just uses a special `BatchSampler` to handle the scheduling and make sure only instances from the same task are batched together.

I'm pretty sure this would work, but I haven't thought through every detail. I also realize it's probably unrealistic to make big changes this late in the game. I don't think this would actually require any API changes though, so we might be able to make these changes later on if it's worth it without breaking backwards compatibility.

Hm, this is currently specific to the basic variant generator (which might be ok), but I have a slightly different approach to discuss:

Currently:

- Categorical samples indices instead of values
- Categorical resolves values in a separate API call

Why?

- Because we actually pre-generate all samples in the `_VariantIterator`

Problems:

- This is not easily generalizable to other searchers
- It's not very easy to overwrite the configs for existing trials, as the Trial.config objects will still have the actual object in them and not the index.


Instead, maybe:

- Scan parameter space for any Categoricals. Replace Categorical.categories with a same-shape list of `_Placeholder<path, index>` objects
- _Placeholder objects could include the string representation of the object (e.g. for the trial table)
- We can choose to only replace objects of types we care about (e.g. Datasets, object refs), and not for primitives
- Create a map of placeholders to "real" categoricals, e.g. `placeholder_to_obj: Dict[_Placeholder, Any]`
- Remember which keys had categoricals, e.g. `categorical_keys: Set[Tuple[str, ...]`
- After sampling, replace every sampled placeholder object with the respective object from `placeholder_to_obj`
- On restore, we only need to update `placeholder_to_obj`

Benefits:
- This won't need any adjustment to the sampling of Categoricals, and it can be wrapped around any searcher.suggest() call. So it generealizes to every searcher.
- Also we can just use the placeholders to update Trial.config objects post restore

For functions, we could either build the replacement map on the fly or just not support it for restoration. I think not supporting it is actually ok.


What do you think?

Fantastic work overall! Your tests are great and your code was easy to follow. As mentioned earlier, I do have a few stylistic critiques and suggestions to make the logic more robust.

Also two general comments:

- Should we emit a message telling the user what we inferred the target version to be as part of `--verbose`? 
- By inferring a default for `--target-version`, Black will never do per-file auto-detection if `project.requires-python` exists and is valid. This is certainly better than the status quo for the lower bound, but it can cause some parsing trouble if the file in question is actually using more modern syntax than specified.[^1]

  Say for example we have `requires-python = ">=3.7"`. If I format a file that's using 3.10's new match statement, Black will fail with a `Cannot parse: ...` error because [Black decides what grammars it'll try from the target version](https://github.com/psf/black/blob/f16333e78ba77ab29ab0b75e10891e6c65c6da7e/src/black/parsing.py#L49-L79). While this keeps things consistent, I don't think it makes for a good user experience. The main two ways to fix this are either to infer a list of all supported target versions or [actually implement this suggestion to make `--target-version` a lower-bound by default](https://github.com/psf/black/issues/751#issuecomment-1168882896). The latter is the long-term fix, but I'm not sure when that will be implemented so the former might be necessary as a temporary fix.[^2]

Thanks again for waiting so patiently <3

[^1]: Also Black would never introduce modern syntax that's not permissible under the project's inferred target version even if the file in question is using more modern syntax -- which is probably fine given explicitly providing `--target-version` works the same way.
[^2]: Unless we decide this is an acceptable failure case and the workaround of explicitly setting `--target-version` is okay -- which is fine by me
